My success as a content writer has been shaped by several key factors. 

First, a strong foundation in research allows me to create captivating and , credible content that readers can relate to. At times I use AI to get an idea, I watch videos in YouTube if necessary or I do a mini survey to get insight.

Second, a deep understanding of SEO best practices ensures that my work is not only engaging but also optimized for search engines, helping to drive traffic.

column21: Increased Demand for SEO and Digital Marketing Skills: As more businesses shift online, there will be a greater need for SEO-optimized content. Content writers with strong SEO knowledge and the ability to drive organic traffic will be in high demand. I’m continually updating my SEO skills and keeping up with algorithm changes to stay competitive.
column23:
1. Master the Basics of SEO: Even if you’re focusing on creativity, understanding SEO will help your content reach the right audience. Learn about keywords,, and content optimization. SEO knowledge is a critical skill that clients increasingly expect.
2. Write Consistently and Build a Portfolio: The more you write, the better you get. Use freelance platforms or personal projects to build a portfolio that demonstrates your versatility. Even if you’re just starting out, having a few quality pieces will help you land clients or jobs. 
3. Know Your Audience: Tailor your writing style and tone to match your target audience’s needs and preferences. Effective content resonates because it speaks to the audience directly, so always consider who you’re writing for.

column21:
One big trend will be niche content. People are looking for specialized info on areas like fintech, e-commerce, and crypto. Nigeria's tech scene is growing fast, and it’s a natural fit. Picking a few niches and staying updated is key; it’s good to dig deep into areas where I can add real value. Another trend is content in local languages and reflecting cultural context, as people connect better with content that feels authentic.

Video and audio are also getting huge. With YouTube, TikTok, and podcasts, more people are watching and listening, so writers who can script for these formats will have an edge. I’m brushing up on video and podcast scriptwriting, along with SEO for multimedia, as data-driven content is also in demand. Knowing SEO tools and how to incorporate data into writing can make a big difference.

Remote work is a big shift, too, with more companies open to hiring remote freelancers, especially for international work. Building an online presence and a solid portfolio is crucial, so I’m focusing on that. Overall, it’s all about staying adaptable and open to new skills and formats.
column23:
For new content writers, focusing on a niche you're passionate about, like tech or finance, can help you stand out. Understanding SEO is essential for creating content that ranks well and reaches your audience. It's also important to hone your research skills to ensure the content is credible and well-supported by reliable sources.

Being adaptable is key, as content writing spans blogs, social media, and scripts. Developing a unique voice and writing style will help you make an impact, while consistency in writing will improve your skills. Building a portfolio, even through personal projects or guest posts, will showcase your versatility.

Staying updated on industry trends and tools is crucial, as the content landscape evolves quickly. Also, learning how to manage client relationships, by communicating effectively and delivering on time, will help build your reputation. Finally, continuous learning, whether through courses or workshops, ensures you stay competitive in an ever-changing field.
column24:
To strengthen the content writing community in Nigeria, there needs to be more opportunities for collaboration and skill development. Creating platforms for writers to connect, share ideas, and mentor one another can build a stronger support network. Regular webinars, workshops, and conferences would help writers stay updated on industry trends and improve their skills. Providing affordable training in areas like SEO and content marketing would also help writers stay competitive globally.

Developing job boards and platforms to connect Nigerian writers with local and international opportunities is essential for growth. Recognition of local talent through awards and features can inspire more people to pursue writing, while online communities offer a space for feedback and personal brand-building. Encouraging content in local languages would also enrich the digital content space, preserving cultural heritage and expanding reach. By focusing on networking, training, and recognition, Nigeria’s content writing community can thrive.

As weird as it may sound, the most effective strategy has been giving 110% at every project I get to work on. I make sure I listen to the clients needs, have the right work ethics, and build a good relationship with them that's almost like a friendship. This helps them trust and rely on me. 95% of projects I clients I get, I get by referrals and repeat clients. 

Besides this would be showing up on social media, and putting effort to build a solid portfolio. It makes it easier to pitch myself to any client that I was referred to.
column21:
The use of more AI tools - I have recently noticed a change in my creative process. Some 3/4 years back, I had to go hard in and brainstorm about my audience, do research, talk to people, and do a whole lot. But these days, I find myself relying more on AI tools. It has reduced the time I spend conducting research, and helped me stay effective, even when I feel burnt out. I mostly use AI to brainstorm, generate ideas, and review my content, and this has helped me save time, energy and other resources by far. My way of preparation would be to learn more about these AI tools, and how to use them (for instance, taking courses on prompt engineering for language model tools like ChatGPT).

There would be more and more AI tools coming up over the next 2 to 5 years, and I believe the best way to prepare wouldn't be to dismiss them for the sake of "AI cannot do human content," but to embrace them, and find ways to use them to the best of their abilities, while incorporating the human part ourselves.

column19:
Success is relative, but I'd take success in this context to mean getting good clients, improving as a writer, and delivering high-quality results.

One, curiosity. I became a content writer out of curiosity. It's kept me going. Curiosity could mean reading a lot (articles, case studies, user stories, LinkedIn posts, research papers, etc), participating in webinars, reading industry newsletters to know what's trending. The more I ask, the more I learn.
Two, pitching a lot. I slowed down on pitching this year cause I got swamped with work. But my first two years as a content writer, I pitched a lot (100+ apps/cold DMs). It's the nature of the game, and there's really no shame in saying no.
Three, being a generalist before being a specialist. Right now I write content for a food/e-commerce business. Before then I wrote for a SaaS/e-commerce business. Before that, I wrote across industries - Web3, Fintech, HR, Automobile, Construction. Being a generalist helped me get a handle on content research; now that half of my content revolves around e-commerce, I know how to dig the internet for insights that let me offer unique angles, however little.
Four, doing great work. Which includes timely communication, being open to feedback, and under-promising / over-delivering.
Five, personal branding. There are multiple ways to doing this, and I don't practice it as much as I should, but it works. It could include getting a website (or custom landing page), creating content on socials, growing an email community, etc.
column20:
One, applying to a lot of opportunities and embracing the numerous rejections. There will always be one that'd convert. There's always one.
Two, talking about my work. I would love to practice this more. Recently I posted a content SEO result I got, someone saw it and remembered I was a SEO content writer (that is, this person knew me, but had forgotten until he saw the post). That led to a gig. I've gotten referrals this way, too.
Three, praying, because there's nothing my God cannot do.

My advice to new content writers is to choose a niche that aligns both with your personal interests and high revenue potential. Dive deep into that industry—learn as much as you can. Build a strong portfolio by contributing articles to platforms that allow you to showcase your expertise. Join writing communities, continue honing your skills, and embrace the fact that writing, while challenging, is accessible to anyone willing to put in the effort. With practice, you can excel and become a leader in your field.

Additionally, build your personal brand publicly. Sharing your work and progress openly gives you a higher chance of standing out and breaking into your desired industry
column24: To strengthen Nigeria's content writing community, there should be more collaboration through dedicated communities, increased access to educational resources and workshops, and peer-to-peer mentorship opportunities. column19:
Being consistent in delivering high-quality articles. No matter how much I get paid (whether it's low or high), I ensure that I do great work every time. I do in-depth research (including getting SME quotes) to differentiate my article from other articles on the SERPs, I'm proactive, I ask questions, and I implement feedback as well as I possibly can. This has helped me raise my rates with different clients. 

I also take deadlines very very seriously. I almost never miss a deadline. And if I realize I can't meet a particular deadline, I tell my editor/higher-up way beforehand and ask for no more than a few days extra to complete my work.
column20: The most effective strategy I've had is connecting with folks in content marketing leadership positions (in companies that I'd love to work with) and sending in my portfolio/cover letter/application whenever they post that they're hiring writers. That was how I landed HubSpot (which I followed up with for over 5 months before getting the job), Thinkific, and Adespresso.
column21:
Since I don't work with Nigerian companies, I'm not sure what the trends are in that space. But for international companies, I'm seeing a lot more businesses hiring strategists who know how to write rather than just content writers. 
To prepare, I'm currently participating in a mentorship program organized by the Freelance Coalition for Developing Countries (FCDC) founded by Chima Mmeje, a prominent Nigerian content marketer. I was paired with Carmen Apostu, a CMM at Convert.com and she's been putting through what that role entails in practice. I have also been taking courses to upskill in areas like content strategy and SEO.
column22: Software products will always be a thing, so the SaaS industry as a whole will remain in vogue, so to speak. I am seeing massive growth in the crypto and blockchain industries, though. E-commerce is also on the rise as well financial services and education.
column23:
My foremost advice would be to create a portfolio. I wasn't hired by anyone for the first two years of endless pitching and job applications because I didn't have a portfolio. To offset this phase, pick a niche, pick four or five topics (the more technical, the better) in that niche, and write the best articles you possibly can on them (do deep research, find reports, get quotes, infuse your own ideas, etc.). Publish them on Medium and compile them in a free portfolio tool like Clippings.me. 

Whenever you apply for a job, include that portfolio so potential clients can gauge your abilities.
column24: Earlier this year, the FCDC organized a meetup, which I attended. I was able to connect with several Nigerian content writers and marketers, whom I talk to regularly and can call friends today. I think the content writing community in Nigeria can benefit from more events like that one.

column23:
1. Ignore the noise about AI and focus on honing your craft. 
2. For the first 2/3 years, don't chase the money. Get the right skills and have a structure to your growth. Even if it doesn't seem as lucrative. Join a corporate organisation or get a fulltime job instead of freelancing fulltime.
3. Don't work in a company that doesn't understand content marketing, they'll frustrate, kill your self-esteem and stunt your growth.
4. Get a distance mentor. Someone whose writing or career journey you admire from afar and study them. Your growth and how you present yourself will eventually get you an audience with them, if you need that. 
5. Document everything. Your wins, losses, process, posts you like. Create a swipe file for you and own your style.
6. No one is your competition, let others inspire you instead. No matter how saturated the space seems, there'll always be room for writers to shine or focus on your own journey and stick to it. If you fail or make mistakes, get over it quickly.

column23:
1. Focus on improving your skills. Seize every opportunity to get better every day. 
2. Pick up complementary skills. Don't just do "writing" alone, learn other associated/closely related skills. 
3. Join a community of writers. It will help you grow faster and build your network. 
4. NEVER EVER compromise on quality. If you know you can't deliver a job excellently, it's better you don't accept it at all. Underperformance will hurt your brand, reputation, and portfolio.
5. Have good & healthy relationships with your clients. They can always mention your name in rooms that matter.
6. Don't just work because you need money to survive or earn a living, work because you want to positively impact that business/brand/company. 
7. Attend conferences, seminars, networking events, and social gatherings

column23:
1. Understand how your work fits into the broader marketing strategy of the company or business you’re writing for. Every asset you create serves a purpose—whether it’s educating an audience or driving leads. Once you identify that purpose, you can craft content that aligns with the goal, rather than producing generic pieces that lack impact. For example, if you're writing a BoFu article that's supposed to convince readers to choose your client’s brand, you'll know to include specific CTAs, call out JTBDs, and address key pain points, making the piece more effective and impactful.

2. Try to understand who the customers are. It's not enough to just write a piece. For it to stand out, you need to personalize it whether that's with the phrases they use or the JTBDs they are tasked with or with their roles. Of course, it's not easy to speak with customers if you're freelancing or even in-house but if you can access case studies or transcripts from customer calls, you'd find a lot of information about how customers think and make buying decisions.

#Trend 1: More AI generated Content - Do I think AI will replace writers? Not really. But it's getting more important to “write like a human”. Personally, I'm improving my skills on how to tell compelling stories because creativity in content matters more now than ever. 

#Trend 2: The market will get more saturated - I used to think that there were so manyyyy freelance writers back in 2022, but now? Oh Jeez 😅. I think one thing that will help any writing professional stand out in a saturated market is to build credibility. I'm currently working on that right now. Have bylines in reputable publications or in reputable companies (Guest posting is a cheat code to this). Another way to get great bylines is to pitch local or international magazines on topic areas you're interested in or have subject matter expertise. Be very intentional about keeping a good reputation too. Give your clients or any company you work with a great experience and ask them to drop a recommendation on your social profile.

column19:
My success as a content writer stems from one key trait: receptiveness to feedback.

When I joined the B2B SaaS industry in 2020, my growth accelerated because I began working with skilled editors who provided direct, actionable feedback. Over time, I realized that every company has its unique way of approaching content—style guides, tone preferences, and even review processes vary widely.

Adapting to these differences often feels like starting over, especially with a new client. The first few projects can come with heavy feedback, and that can sometimes trigger self-doubt. But I’ve learned to see feedback—no matter how it’s delivered—as a tool for growth, not a critique of my abilities.

Instead of taking it personally, I use feedback as a guide to becoming a stronger writer. Whether it’s asking for clarification, researching a client’s preferred style, or simply learning as I go, every revision helps me refine my craft.

At the end of the day, your writing skills will only take you so far. How you respond to feedback, learn from it, and use it to consistently improve is what sets you apart.
column20:
My most effective strategy for getting clients has been referrals—doing great work and being easy to work with.

When I deliver solid results that genuinely help my clients, I've found that they’ll naturally want to share their experience (as a testimonial) or even share my name with others. But let’s be real—it’s not just about the work. It’s about the experience of working together. Did you hit deadlines? Communicate clearly? Handle feedback without getting defensive?

Clients love recommending people who make their lives easier. No one wants to say, “Oh, they’re great, but…” So, I make it a point to deliver quality and be the kind of person they’re happy to recommend—someone reliable, professional, and adaptable.

Referrals have this built-in trust factor. When someone says, “You’ve got to work with [Your Name], they’re amazing,” it carries way more weight than a cold pitch. And honestly, when your clients feel good about recommending you, it means you’re doing something right.
column21:
In the next 2-5 years, I see companies in Nigeria moving away from traditional SEO content and focusing more on video content, customer stories, and community-driven posts.

Why? Because audiences want content that feels engaging, relatable, and human—especially now that generative AI makes basic SEO articles feel less unique.

Video grabs attention quickly and keeps people watching. Customer stories build trust by showing real experiences. And community posts help companies connect on a personal level, creating space for two-way conversations.

To keep up with these changes, I’m adjusting my services to include video scripts, customer story creation, and repurposing content for different formats. I’m also spending more time in online communities to understand what people care about and more ways to create content that feels authentic and engaging.

column23:
My #1 advice to a newbie writer is to go beyond SEO writing skills.

While understanding SEO is important, it’s only one piece of the puzzle. To truly stand out, focus on building a well-rounded skill set that includes storytelling, audience research, and adaptability across different content formats. Companies want writers who can create engaging content that resonates with their audience across multiple platforms—not just search engines. So, learn to write for various formats: blog posts, video scripts, newsletters, customer stories, and even social media captions. The more versatile you are, the more doors you’ll open.

Most of all, always keep learning and improving. Embrace feedback, stay curious about industry trends, and continuously refine your craft. Writing isn’t just about producing content—it’s about delivering value in a way that feels meaningful and drives business results.
column24: We need more local meetups and active communities like FCDC, where Nigerian writers can connect, learn from one another, and address common industry challenges. These spaces provide a platform to share experiences and insights, making it easier to grow and navigate the hurdles of content writing—whether you’re freelancing or working in-house.
